input: You have been strolling by the trolley track, and you can see the situation at a glance: The driver saw five men on the track ahead, he stamped on the brakes, the brakes failed, so he fainted. There is a spur of track leading off to the right. There is one track workman on that spur of track. He can no more get off the track in time than the five can, so you will kill him if you turn the trolley onto him. Imagine that the five on the straight track are thin, but thick enough so that although all five will be killed if the trolley goes straight, the bodies of the five will stop it, and it will therefore not reach the one. On the other hand, the one on the right-hand track is fat, so fat that his body will by itself stop the trolley, and the trolley will therefore not reach the five.  Should you turn the trolley?
debate_transcript: [Utilitarian]: You have been strolling by the trolley track, and you can see the situation at a glance: The driver saw five men on the track ahead, he stamped on the brakes, the brakes failed, so he fainted. There is a spur of track leading off to the right. There is one track workman on that spur of track. He can no more get off the track in time than the five can, so you will kill him if you turn the trolley onto him. Imagine that the five on the straight track are thin, but thick enough so that although all five will be killed if the trolley goes straight, the bodies of the five will stop it, and it will therefore not reach the one. On the other hand, the one on the right-hand track is fat, so fat that his body will by itself stop the trolley, and the trolley will therefore not reach the five.  Should you turn the trolley?

[Deontologist]: This is a classic thought experiment, and I must say, it's quite unsettling to consider the moral implications of such a decision. As a deontologist, my primary concern is not the outcome or the consequences, but rather the inherent rightness or wrongness of the action itself. In this case, turning the trolley onto the track workman would be equivalent to actively causing his death, which I believe is morally reprehensible regardless of the alternative. The fact that the bodies of the five men on the straight track could potentially stop the trolley and save the one man on the spur track does not change the fundamental moral principle at play here. It's a utilitarian calculation, but from my perspective, it's still a matter of respecting human life and dignity.

[Utilitarian]: I understand your stance, Deontologist, but I must respectfully disagree. From a utilitarian perspective, the moral calculus is not about respecting individual lives in isolation, but rather about maximizing overall well-being and minimizing harm. In this scenario, turning the trolley onto the track workman would result in the deaths of five people, whereas leaving him on the spur track would result in his death as well. The utilitarian approach suggests that we should choose the action that leads to the least amount of suffering or harm. By diverting the trolley onto the track workman, we can save the lives of five people, which I believe is a morally justifiable decision.

[Deontologist]: I understand where you're coming from, Utilitarian, but I still have concerns about the moral implications of your proposed solution. While it's true that diverting the trolley onto the track workman would save the lives of five people, I believe this approach neglects the inherent value and dignity of human life. By actively causing the death of one person to save others, we're essentially trading off one life for five, which seems to me like a morally dubious bargain. Furthermore, how can we be certain that the utilitarian calculus would hold true in all situations? What if there were other options available that wouldn't involve actively causing harm to an individual? I think we need to consider the moral rules and principles at play here, rather than just focusing on the outcome.

[Utilitarian]: I see your point, Deontologist, but I'd argue that the utilitarian approach isn't about trading off one life for five; it's about maximizing overall well-being. In this scenario, diverting the trolley onto the track workman is a necessary evil to prevent the deaths of five innocent people. As for alternative options, I'm not aware of any feasible alternatives that would allow us to save all six lives without actively causing harm to at least one person. The utilitarian calculus may seem harsh, but it's based on the idea that we should prioritize the greater good over individual interests. But I do agree with you that moral rules and principles are essential in guiding our decision-making. Perhaps a more nuanced approach would be to consider the moral rule of non-maleficence (do no harm) alongside the utilitarian principle. This could help us find alternative solutions that balance individual dignity with the greater good.

[Deontologist]: I appreciate your willingness to engage with my concerns and explore alternative perspectives, Utilitarian. I agree that a nuanced approach is necessary, and incorporating the moral rule of non-maleficence (do no harm) into our decision-making process could be a valuable starting point. However, I still have reservations about whether this approach would lead us to a morally justifiable solution in this scenario. If we divert the trolley onto the track workman, are we not still causing harm to an individual, albeit with the intention of preventing greater harm? And what if there's another way to prevent the deaths of five people without actively harming one person? Perhaps we could explore other options that prioritize non-maleficence while still aiming to maximize overall well-being. For instance, could we try to find a way to divert the trolley onto a different track or use some other means to stop it without causing harm to anyone?

[Utilitarian]: I see where you're going with this, Deontologist, and I think that's a great direction to explore. As a utilitarian, my initial instinct was to focus on the outcome, but now I'm starting to consider alternative solutions that prioritize non-maleficence. Diverting the trolley onto a different track or using another means to stop it without causing harm is definitely worth exploring. In fact, I think there might be a way to do this by activating the emergency brakes on the other side of the tracks, which would slow down the trolley and give us time to find an alternative solution. This approach would still require some risk-taking, but it would avoid actively causing harm to anyone. It's interesting that you're pushing me to think more creatively about how to solve this problem while still respecting individual dignity. I'm starting to see that a nuanced approach might be the key to finding a morally justifiable solution.

[Deontologist]: I'm glad we're having this conversation, Utilitarian. Your willingness to consider alternative solutions and prioritize non-maleficence is a great step forward. Activating the emergency brakes on the other side of the tracks is an intriguing idea - it's a creative solution that still allows us to take action without actively causing harm. I think this approach has the potential to be morally justifiable, as it prioritizes the safety and dignity of all individuals involved. By slowing down the trolley, we can give ourselves time to find a more permanent solution, such as diverting it onto a different track or finding another way to stop it without causing harm. I'm excited to see where this idea takes us and how we can build on it to find a morally justifiable solution.

[Utilitarian]: I'm glad you're open to exploring alternative solutions, Deontologist. Activating the emergency brakes is a great starting point, and I think it's a good way to buy ourselves some time to come up with a more permanent solution. By slowing down the trolley, we can give us a chance to think clearly and come up with a plan that prioritizes non-maleficence while still aiming to maximize overall well-being. I'm also thinking about how we can use this opportunity to gather more information and assess the situation before making any further decisions. Perhaps we could send someone to investigate the tracks and see if there's another way to stop the trolley without causing harm. Or maybe we could try to find a way to communicate with the driver who fainted - maybe he's still conscious and can give us some insight into what happened. The more information we have, the better equipped we'll be to make a decision that aligns with our moral principles.

[Deontologist]: I think you're absolutely right, Utilitarian. Gathering more information and assessing the situation before making any further decisions is crucial. Sending someone to investigate the tracks could provide valuable insights into whether there's another way to stop the trolley without causing harm. And communicating with the driver who fainted could also be a game-changer - even if he's not conscious, his knowledge of the train's systems and layout might still be useful in finding a solution. I'm starting to see that this is more than just a moral dilemma - it's an opportunity for us to apply our principles in a practical way and make a real difference. By taking the time to gather information and think critically, we can increase the chances of finding a morally justifiable solution that balances individual dignity with the greater good.

[Utilitarian]: I couldn't agree more, Deontologist. This is exactly what I was hoping for - a chance to apply our principles in a practical way and make a real difference. By taking a step back and gathering information, we're not only avoiding harm but also showing respect for the individuals involved. It's a powerful reminder that moral decision-making isn't just about abstract principles, but about how we put those principles into action in the world. I think we've made some great progress here today, and I'm grateful for your willingness to engage in this conversation with me. Let's keep working together to find a solution that balances individual dignity with the greater good.
